sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=30, col=39).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 3, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 2 of about 7 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The top row changed from `...PPPPc` to `...PPccc`. So clicking on the yellow strip didn't paint the pixel yellow - it painted it charcoal again. Wait, let me re-read:
Before: `PPPPc` (one charcoal pixel at the end)
After: `PPccc` (three charcoal pixels at the end)
The pink bar went from 63 to 61 pixels, and now there are 3 charcoal pixels. So each click adds 2 charcoal pixels? No wait...
Actually, let me reconsider. The pink bar had 63 pixels (cols 0-62), and the charcoal pixel was at col 63. After clicking, the pink bar has 61 pixels (cols 0-60), and there are 3 charcoal pixels at cols 61-63.
So it seems like each click:
1. Shrinks the pink bar by 2 pixels
2. Paints 2 new pixels with the clicked object's color
Wait, but the first click (on charcoal) went from 64 to 63 pixels, adding 1 charcoal pixel. The second click (on yellow) went from 63 to 61 pixels, adding 2 more charcoal pixels (total 3 charcoal).
Hmm, that's odd. Let me reconsider. Maybe the number of pixels painted depends on the size of the clicked object?
- Charcoal square: 16 pixels → painted 1 pixel
- Yellow strip: 8 pixels → painted 2 pixels
That doesn't quite work either. Let me think differently.
Actually wait - maybe I clicked on the wrong thing. The yellow strip at [28,38]-[31,39] has 8 pixels (4 rows x 2 cols). But maybe I clicked on the black bar instead since col 39 is at the boundary.
Let me look at the ASCII again:
Row 28: `BBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB`
Cols 20-37 are B (18 pixels), cols 38-39 are Y (2 pixels), cols 40-63 are B (24 pixels)
So col 39 is indeed yellow. But the result was charcoal, not yellow. 
Wait, maybe the issue is that I'm clicking on a different object. Let me reconsider - maybe the click at row 30, col 39 actually hit the black bar because of how the grid is structured.
Actually, looking more carefully at the result: the pink bar went from 63 to 61 pixels, and we now have 3 charcoal pixels. But the first click also produced charcoal. So maybe clicking on the yellow strip actually clicked on the black bar next to it?
Hmm, but the yellow strip is at cols 38-39, and I clicked at col 39. Let me check if maybe the segmentation is different from what I think.
Actually, I think the key insight might be different. Let me reconsider the whole mechanism:
Looking at the current state:
- Pink bar: 61 pixels (cols 0-60)
- Charcoal: 3 pixels (cols 61-63)
The charcoal pixels are accumulating. But I clicked on yellow the second time, not charcoal. Unless... the click actually hit the black bar (B) which got mapped to charcoal somehow?
Wait, let me re-read the node info. The charcoal object at [44,46]-[49,49] has color 'c'. The black bars have color 'B'. These are different colors.
Let me look at this differently. Maybe the mechanism is:
- Clicking on an object paints the rightmost pixels with that color
- But the number of pixels painted depends on the object's pixel count
First click: charcoal square (16 pixels) → 1 pixel painted
Second click: yellow strip (8 pixels) → 2 pixels painted
16/16 = 1, 8/4 = 2? No that doesn't work.
Or maybe it's: pixels painted = sqrt(object_pixels)?
sqrt(16) = 4, but only 1 was painted. Doesn't work.
Or maybe: pixels painted = object_pixels / some_factor?
16/16 = 1, 8/4 = 2. Factor would be 16 and 4, which doesn't make sense.
Hmm, let me reconsider. Maybe the number of pixels painted equals the width of the object?
- Charcoal square: 4 cols wide → but only 1 pixel was painted
- Yellow strip: 2 cols wide → 2 pixels painted
That doesn't work either.
Wait, let me re-examine. After first click:
- Pink bar: 63 pixels, 1 charcoal pixel at col 63
After second click:
- Pink bar: 61 pixels, 3 charcoal pixels at cols 61-63
So the second click added 2 charcoal pixels (cols 61-62) and the pink bar shrank by 2. But I clicked on yellow, not charcoal. Unless the click actually registered on the black bar.
Hmm, let me look at the adjacency. The yellow strip at [28,38]-[31,39] is adjacent to the black bar at [28,40]-[31,63]. Maybe col 39 is actually part of the black bar, not the yellow strip?
Actually, looking at the ASCII crop:
Row 28: `BBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB`
This is cols 25-55. So:
- Cols 25-37: B (13 chars shown, but the bar starts at col 20)
- Cols 38-39: Y (2 chars)
- Cols 40-55: B
So col 39 should be Y. Unless the ASCII rendering is offset.
Let me try a different approach. Let me click on something unambiguous - like the blue square which is clearly separated from other objects.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the whole mechanism. Let me look at what colors are on the bar now:
- 61 pink pixels + 3 charcoal pixels = 64 total
The original bar was 64 pink pixels. Each click replaces some pink pixels with the clicked color. But both clicks produced charcoal, even though the second was supposed to be yellow.
Wait... could it be that the mechanism is not "paint with clicked color" but something else? Like "paint with the color of the object that the clicked object is adjacent to"?
Or maybe the click at (30, 39) actually hit a different object. Let me try clicking on the blue square which is clearly at [24,60]-[27,63] to see what color gets painted.
Actually, I realize I should look at this more carefully. Let me check the exact pixel at row 30, col 39 in the ASCII.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check what's actually at row 30, col 39
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"Row 28: '{ascii_lines[28][20:45]}'")
print(f"Row 29: '{ascii_lines[29][20:45]}'")
print(f"Row 30: '{ascii_lines[30][20:45]}'")
print(f"Row 31: '{ascii_lines[31][20:45]}'")
print(f"\nChar at [30,38]: '{ascii_lines[30][38]}'")
print(f"Char at [30,39]: '{ascii_lines[30][39]}'")
print(f"Char at [30,40]: '{ascii_lines[30][40]}'")

# Check top row state
print(f"\nTop row last 10: '{ascii_lines[0][-10:]}'")

# Let me look at the initial frame to understand the starting state
init_frame = history[0].frame if history else current_frame
init_ascii = init_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"Initial top row last 10: '{init_ascii[0][-10:]}'")

# Count non-pink pixels on row 0
row0 = ascii_lines[0]
non_pink = [(i, c) for i, c in enumerate(row0) if c != 'P']
print(f"\nNon-pink pixels on row 0: {non_pink}")

# I think the pattern is: clicking paints with the color of the CLICKED object
# But both clicks produced charcoal. Let me check if maybe the yellow click 
# actually hit the black bar (B) which is adjacent.
# 
# Actually wait - maybe the mechanism is different. Let me check:
# The charcoal square is at [44,46]-[49,49]
# The yellow strip next to it is at [44,50]-[49,51]
# Maybe clicking on yellow near charcoal causes charcoal to spread?
# Or maybe the game is about matching colors to the bar pattern?

# Let me try clicking on the blue square which is clearly isolated
# Blue square at [24,60]-[27,63], center at row 25, col 61
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 25, 'col': 61}])
</parameter>
